name: Arc de Triomphe
thenCaption: Paris, 1836 — the arch crowns the axe between the old city and Haussmann's future boulevards.
nowCaption: Today — traffic still circles the star of twelve avenues; wealth radiates west.
summary: Commissioned by Napoleon after Austerlitz, the Arc de Triomphe became the ceremonial hinge of Haussmann's radial plan. It celebrates imperial victory while anchoring a geometry that later prioritized automobile flow over neighborhood life.
planningRole: Monumental axis · imperial symbolism · traffic engineering
inequalityAngle: The view from the Arc frames the westward gradient of Parisian wealth; eastern and northern arrondissements remain largely off the postcard.
creditNow: Wikimedia Commons · Benh LIEU SONG, CC BY-SA

name: Tour Eiffel
thenCaption: 1889 — temporary iron tower for the World's Fair on the Champ de Mars.
nowCaption: Today — global icon; ticketed ascent; luxury apartments in the 7e with views.
summary: Built as a temporary exhibit for the 1889 Exposition, the Eiffel Tower was meant to demonstrate French engineering prowess. It stayed, defined the skyline, and turned the Champ de Mars corridor into a high-value tourist and residential frontier.
planningRole: World's Fair infrastructure · skyline landmark · tourism anchor
inequalityAngle: Tourism revenue and view premiums concentrate in adjacent western arrondissements while peripheral housing estates remain outside the frame.

name: Basilique du Sacré-Cœur
planner: Paul Abadie; national vow after Franco-Prussian War
thenCaption: Montmartre, early 1900s — white basilica rising above working-class hillside.
nowCaption: Today — overlooks a gentrifying 18e; crowds on the steps every sunset.
summary: After France's defeat in 1870, a national vow funded this basilica atop Montmartre — a moral monument imposed on a rebellious, working-class hill. Its visibility from every corner of Paris made the periphery legible as both sacred and surveilled.
planningRole: National monument · hilltop landmark · tourism gentrification
inequalityAngle: Montmartre's artists' colony became a luxury destination; the basilica marks the moment state religion met speculative real estate.

name: Palais Garnier (Opéra)
thenCaption: 1875 — Haussmann's 'new Rome' for opera on a cleared Right Bank block.
nowCaption: Today — still elite culture; golden foyer, €100+ tickets, luxury retail nearby.
summary: Napoleon III wanted a temple to the arts that would rival any European capital. Garnier's opera house anchored the Grands Boulevards — a deliberate mixing of culture, consumption, and class display in the rebuilt center.
planningRole: Cultural flagship · Haussmann centerpiece · luxury district anchor
inequalityAngle: The Opéra district prices out the working audiences it was once meant to elevate; culture becomes a land-value amenity.

name: Panthéon
thenCaption: Revolutionary Paris — church reborn as temple to the nation.
nowCaption: Today — Latin Quarter landmark amid universities and rising rents.
summary: Originally Sainte-Geneviève, the Panthéon was reconsecrated to house the nation's great men — a republican monument in the scholarly Latin Quarter. Its dome dominates a district that has long mixed students, state institutions, and expensive housing.
inequalityAngle: The 5e scores high on culture but median income is middling — knowledge workers and tourists, not the wealthiest Parisians.

name: Place de la République
thenCaption: Third Republic — new square where medieval blocks had been cleared.
nowCaption: Today — protest stage, skateboards, renovation 2013 pedestrian plaza.
summary: Haussmann's planners widened this junction into a republican square crowned by Marianne. It became eastern Paris's public living room — protests, gatherings, and now a pedestrianized plaza after a 2013 redesign.
planningRole: Civic square · protest space · pedestrianization pilot
inequalityAngle: A rare eastern space claimed for public assembly; gentrification in the 3e/10e/11e triangle pressures who feels at home here.

name: Bibliothèque nationale (François-Mitterrand)
thenCaption: 1990s — four glass towers like open books on a former industrial riverside.
nowCaption: Today — national library; Seine promenade; ongoing eastern renewal debates.
summary: Mitterrand's Grands Projets placed the national library in the 13e, betting on east-bank renewal. Four L-shaped towers evoke open books but also isolated grandeur on a formerly industrial waterfront.
planningRole: Grand Projet · state cultural investment · east-bank regeneration
inequalityAngle: The 13e gained prestige architecture but remains below western median income — symbolic planning without full gentrification.

name: Centre Pompidou
planner: Renzo Piano, Richard Rogers, Gianfranco Franchini
thenCaption: 1977 — exposed ducts shock the Marais; Beaubourg slum cleared.
nowCaption: Today — museum plaza; street performers; Marais among Paris's priciest.
summary: President Pompidou's modern art center inverted architecture — structure and services on the outside. Built after clearing the Beaubourg market district, it catalyzed the Marais's transformation from working-class to global chic.
planningRole: Modernist cultural center · slum clearance · gentrification engine
inequalityAngle: Culture-led renewal that made the 4e desirable for wealth; long-term residents displaced by galleries and Airbnbs.
